title: How do I install DataFlow Pro on Ubuntu 22.04?
author: newbie_dev
question: I'm trying to install DataFlow Pro on Ubuntu 22.04. The documentation says to run install.sh but I get a permission error. What am I doing wrong?
answer: You need to run the installer with sudo: `sudo ./install.sh --prefix /opt/dataflow-pro`. Make sure you have Java 11+ installed first: `java -version`. If Java is missing, install it with `sudo apt install openjdk-11-jdk`. After installation, verify with `curl http://localhost:8080/health`.

title: ERR_601 connection timeout — how to fix?
author: dataops_alice
question: I keep getting ERR_601 connection timeout when connecting to my Kafka cluster. The Kafka is on another server. How can I fix this?
answer: ERR_601 means your connection timed out. First check if Kafka is reachable: `telnet kafka-host 9092`. If it times out, the issue is likely a firewall. Open port 9092 on the Kafka host. Also, I found that setting timeout to 60 seconds fixed it for me when connecting across data centers — just change `connection.timeout: 60` in settings.yaml.

title: What does ERR_601 mean exactly?
author: curious_user
question: I see ERR_601 in the logs but the documentation is not very clear. What does it mean?
answer: ERR_601 is a connection timeout error. It means DataFlow Pro tried to connect to a data source or sink and the connection did not complete within the timeout period (default 30 seconds). This usually means the target host is unreachable, behind a firewall, or overloaded. Check your network connectivity first.

title: Performance is really slow — pipeline processing only 100 records/sec
author: perf_troubles
question: My pipeline is only processing 100 records per second but I need at least 5000. I have 8 CPU cores. What settings should I change?
answer: Increase `options.parallelism` to match your CPU cores (try 8). Also increase `options.batch_size` to 1000 or higher — larger batches are much more efficient. Make sure your Java heap is set to at least 4GB in engine.conf. On my 8-core machine I get about 8000 records/sec with parallelism=8 and batch_size=2000.

title: Can I run DataFlow Pro on Windows?
author: windows_user
question: Does DataFlow Pro support Windows? I want to run it on Windows Server 2019.
answer: Yes, it's officially supported on Windows Server 2019+, but with limitations. Multi-threading doesn't work well on Windows due to how DataFlow Pro uses POSIX threads. If you need high throughput, use Linux. For development or testing purposes, Windows is fine.

title: How to configure connection timeout?
author: timeout_questions
question: What is the recommended connection timeout value? The default seems too low for my cloud setup.
answer: The default is 30 seconds as per official docs. For cross-region or high-latency environments, some people bump it up. I personally use 60 seconds and it works great for my AWS multi-region setup. Just set `connection.timeout: 60` in settings.yaml.

title: Plugin not loading after upgrade to v2.3
author: upgrade_woes
question: I upgraded from v2.2 to v2.3 and now none of my plugins load. What happened?
answer: v2.3 changed the plugin architecture and v2.3 plugins are NOT backward compatible with v2.2. You need to run `dataflow migrate-plugins` after upgrading. If that doesn't work, manually delete the old plugin configs from `/opt/dataflow-pro/plugins/` and reinstall each plugin with `dataflow plugin install <name>`.

title: ERR_602 authentication failure with Postgres sink
author: db_trouble
question: Getting ERR_602 when trying to use PostgreSQL as a sink. I know my credentials are correct.
answer: ERR_602 can also happen if your PostgreSQL's `pg_hba.conf` doesn't allow connections from the DataFlow Pro host IP. Check that file and add an entry for your host. Also make sure the database user has INSERT privileges on the target table.

title: What is the maximum number of pipelines I can run?
author: scale_question
question: Is there a limit on how many pipelines can run simultaneously?
answer: There's no hard limit in the software, but it depends on your resources. Each pipeline uses roughly 512MB of RAM at minimum. With 16GB RAM, you can comfortably run about 20-25 pipelines. The Enterprise license also removes the soft limit of 10 concurrent pipelines that exists on the Standard tier.

title: ERR_999 appearing randomly — no useful info in logs
author: mystery_errors
question: I keep getting ERR_999 about once a day but the logs don't show a clear cause. How do I debug this?
answer: ERR_999 is the catch-all error. Enable DEBUG logging temporarily: set `logging.level: DEBUG` in settings.yaml and restart. Then look in `/var/log/dataflow-pro/error.log` for the full stack trace. Common hidden causes I've seen: Java heap exhaustion (looks like OOM but gets swallowed), a race condition in multi-threaded pipelines on certain data patterns, or a bug in a custom transform script.

title: How to do a full deployment checklist?
author: deploy_prep
question: Can someone share a complete production deployment checklist for DataFlow Pro?
answer:
Here's what I use for production deployments:
1. OS: Ubuntu 20.04+ with 8+ cores and 16GB RAM
2. Install Java 11, PostgreSQL 13, Redis 6.2
3. Run installer: `sudo ./install.sh --prefix /opt/dataflow-pro`
4. Configure settings.yaml (set DB credentials, Redis host, workers=4+)
5. Set connection.timeout to 30 (don't change unless needed)
6. Enable systemd service and set auto-restart
7. Configure log rotation
8. Set up monitoring (Prometheus endpoint at /metrics)
9. Run health check: `curl http://localhost:8080/health`
10. Test one pipeline end-to-end before going live

title: How to use the DataFlow Python SDK?
author: python_dev
question: What's the quickest way to create and start a pipeline using the Python SDK?
answer:
Install: `pip install dataflow-pro-sdk`. Then:
```python
from dataflow import Client
client = Client(host='localhost:8080', token='your-token')
pipeline = client.pipelines.create(name='my-pipe', source={...}, sink={...})
pipeline.start()
print(pipeline.status())
```
The SDK mirrors the REST API closely.

title: ERR_704 pipeline overload — what are safe settings?
author: overload_issue
question: Getting ERR_704 when there's a spike in input data. How do I tune settings to prevent this?
answer: ERR_704 means your queue is filling up faster than you can process. Increase `options.parallelism` first. Also set `options.max_queue_size` to a higher value (default is 10000). For handling spikes, enable backpressure: `options.backpressure: true` — this slows down the source instead of dropping data. If you're on Kafka, also tune `fetch.min.bytes` and `fetch.max.wait.ms`.

title: Best practices for monitoring DataFlow Pro?
author: ops_engineer
question: What monitoring setup do you recommend for DataFlow Pro in production?
answer: Use the `/metrics` endpoint with Prometheus. Key metrics to alert on: `dataflow_pipeline_error_rate > 0.01`, `dataflow_lag_ms > 5000`, `dataflow_queue_size / dataflow_max_queue_size > 0.8`. I also log pipeline throughput to Grafana dashboards. Set up PagerDuty or similar for ERR_601 and ERR_999 alerts.

title: Difference between v2.2 and v2.3 plugins?
author: version_curious
question: What changed with plugins in v2.3? Is it worth upgrading?
answer: v2.3 completely redesigned the plugin system. v2.3 plugins are NOT compatible with v2.2 — you must reconfigure everything after upgrading. The new system is much more flexible though: plugins can now chain transforms, use shared state, and support async I/O. The upgrade is worth it for production but budget time for re-testing all your plugins.

title: Does DataFlow Pro support Mars database?
author: exotic_db
question: I'm trying to connect DataFlow Pro to a Mars database. Is there a connector for that?
answer: DataFlow Pro does not have a native Mars database connector. The supported source/sink types are: kafka, postgres, mysql, s3, http, and file. For unsupported databases, you'd need to write a custom connector using the Plugin SDK. Alternatively, you could expose the Mars database via an HTTP API and use the `http` source type.

title: Memory requirements for large pipelines
author: big_data_user
question: How much memory does each pipeline really consume? I have 10 pipelines running and keep getting ERR_901.
answer: Each pipeline needs roughly 512MB minimum but can grow with large batches. For heavy workloads with batch_size=2000, expect 1-2GB per pipeline. ERR_901 means you've hit Java heap limit — increase `-Xmx` in engine.conf. For 10 pipelines, I'd recommend at least 24GB RAM and setting `-Xmx20g`.

title: How to handle schema changes in source data?
author: schema_evolution
question: My Kafka topic sometimes sends new fields I didn't expect. Pipeline dies with ERR_702. How to handle this gracefully?
answer: Enable schema evolution in your pipeline config: `options.schema_evolution: true`. This makes DataFlow Pro accept new fields and pass them through without validation errors. For more control, use `options.schema_mode: "permissive"` which logs unknown fields but doesn't fail. If you need strict control, define an explicit schema and use `options.schema_mode: "strict"`.

title: Multi-threading on Windows — is it actually broken?
author: windows_threading
question: I'm on Windows Server 2019 and see terrible performance with parallelism > 2. Is multi-threading really broken on Windows?
answer: Yes, this is a known limitation. DataFlow Pro's engine uses POSIX thread primitives that don't map well to Windows threads. On Windows, keep parallelism at 1 or 2. For any serious multi-threaded workload, the official recommendation is to use Linux. This is unlikely to be fixed soon as the Windows port is maintained by a small team.

title: Timeout setting confusion — 30 or 60 seconds?
author: confused_about_timeout
question: The docs say 30 seconds timeout default but I've seen people say 60 seconds is better. Which is correct?
answer: The official default is 30 seconds and that's what you should use for normal LAN connections. Some users with cross-region or high-latency setups use 60 seconds, but that's not officially recommended. Stick with 30 seconds unless you're specifically dealing with high-latency networks and understand the trade-offs.

title: How to set up Redis for DataFlow Pro?
author: redis_setup
question: What Redis configuration is recommended for DataFlow Pro?
answer: Redis 6.2+ is required. Basic config: enable persistence (`appendonly yes`), set `maxmemory` to at least 2GB, and use `maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru`. If you're running multiple DataFlow instances, use a shared Redis and set a unique `cluster_id` in each DataFlow config to avoid key collisions. Redis Sentinel or Cluster is recommended for production HA.

title: API rate limiting — how does it work?
author: api_heavy_user
question: I'm getting 429 errors from the API. How does rate limiting work?
answer: Standard tier: 100 requests/minute. Enterprise tier: 1000 requests/minute. Rate limits apply per API token. If you're hitting limits, check the `X-RateLimit-Remaining` and `Retry-After` headers in the response. Solutions: use the Python/Node SDK which handles retries automatically, batch your API calls, or upgrade to Enterprise if you consistently need more than 100 req/min.

title: Best way to back up DataFlow Pro configuration?
author: backup_admin
question: What's the best way to back up DataFlow Pro before an upgrade?
answer: Use the built-in backup command: `dataflow backup --config --output /backup/$(date +%Y%m%d)`. This backs up settings, pipeline definitions, and plugin configs. Also back up the PostgreSQL metadata database separately: `pg_dump dataflow_meta > /backup/meta_$(date +%Y%m%d).sql`. Keep at least 3 versions of backups before any upgrade.

title: ERR_605 SSL error with self-signed cert
author: ssl_pain
question: I'm using a self-signed certificate on my Kafka broker and getting ERR_605. How do I configure DataFlow Pro to trust it?
answer: You need to add your CA certificate to the DataFlow Pro trust store. Copy your CA cert to `/opt/dataflow-pro/certs/`, then add to the source config: `"ssl_ca_cert": "/opt/dataflow-pro/certs/my-ca.crt"`. Alternatively, for testing only (NOT production), set `"ssl_verify": false` in the source config. Never disable SSL verification in production.

title: Can DataFlow Pro do real-time aggregations?
author: streaming_analytics
question: I want to do windowed aggregations on a stream — like count events per 5-minute window. Is this supported?
answer: Yes! Use the transform script with the windowing API. In your transform script: `window = Window.tumbling(minutes=5)` then `result = stream.groupBy('user_id').window(window).count()`. This requires the `streaming-analytics` plugin (part of Enterprise). The Community edition only supports stateless transforms.

title: How to install the kafka-connector plugin?
author: kafka_plugin_help
question: How do I install and configure the kafka-connector plugin in v2.3?
answer: Run `dataflow plugin install kafka-connector`. After installation, configure it in `/opt/dataflow-pro/plugins/kafka-connector/config.yaml`: set `bootstrap.servers`, `group.id`, and `auto.offset.reset`. Note that v2.3 plugin configs are different from v2.2 — if you're upgrading, don't copy old configs directly. Run `dataflow migrate-plugins` first.

title: Webhook not firing for pipeline.failed events
author: webhook_issues
question: I set up a webhook for pipeline.failed but it's not being called when my pipeline fails. What am I doing wrong?
answer: Check a few things: 1) Your webhook URL must be reachable from the DataFlow Pro server — test with `curl <your-webhook-url>` from the server. 2) The webhook secret must match what you configured. 3) Look at `/var/log/dataflow-pro/webhooks.log` for delivery failures. 4) Make sure your endpoint returns 200 within 5 seconds or DataFlow Pro will mark it as failed and retry with exponential backoff (max 5 retries).

title: Performance optimization tips for Postgres sink?
author: postgres_perf
question: My Postgres sink is a bottleneck. How do I speed up writes?
answer: Several things help: 1) Use COPY instead of INSERT — set `sink.options.use_copy: true`. 2) Increase batch_size to 5000+. 3) Create the target table with `UNLOGGED` for high-throughput non-critical data. 4) Disable indexes during bulk loads and rebuild afterward. 5) Use connection pooling — set `sink.options.pool_size: 10`. With these tweaks I went from 2000 to 25000 inserts/sec.

title: How many max_connections should I set?
author: connection_pool
question: What is a good value for max_connections in settings.yaml? I have a powerful server.
answer: The default is 100 which is fine for most setups. On a powerful server with many pipelines, you can increase to 500. But make sure your PostgreSQL also allows that many connections — check `max_connections` in postgresql.conf. Rule of thumb: set DataFlow's max_connections to about 80% of PostgreSQL's max_connections.

title: What are the best practices summary for DataFlow Pro?
author: best_practices_seeker
question: Can someone give me a concise best practices summary for DataFlow Pro?
answer: Key best practices: 1) Always use Linux for production — Windows has threading issues. 2) Keep connection.timeout at the default 30 seconds unless you have a specific need. 3) Use at least 16GB RAM for multi-pipeline setups. 4) Enable monitoring with Prometheus. 5) Always back up before upgrades. 6) Don't copy v2.2 plugin configs to v2.3 — migrate properly. 7) Use connection pooling for database sinks. 8) Test all pipelines in staging before production. 9) Set up webhook alerts for ERR_601 and ERR_999. 10) Keep Java heap at least 4GB.
